Skip to contents
mixedsubjectsirt 0.0.1
  • Reference
    • Per-Item Lambda (Experimental)
    • Choosing Lambda in Mixed-Subjects IRT
    • IRT Linking and Gradient Asymmetry: Diagnostic Guide
    • Mixed-Subjects 1PL Calibration
    • Mixed-Subjects IRT Calibration
    • Simulation Validation of the Mixed-Subjects MML Estimator

Changes and Findings: 2026-06-04

Overview

This document records the Phase 0 diagnostic results and Phase 1 implementation changes for the mixedsubjectsirt package. It follows the expert reviewer feedback received on 2026-06-03 identifying three separable causes of λ = 0 and recommending a marginal-MML implementation as the principal fix.

CRITICAL FIX (2026-06-05): quadrature variance was 2, not 1

Discovered by the coverage simulation (Scenario 2). The simulation exposed near-zero coverage for discrimination Wald intervals while intercept coverage was nominal. Diagnosis:

  1. SE calibration was perfect — se_over_empsd ≈ 1.0 for every parameter, so the Louis bread was correct.
  2. Discriminations were biased downward ~28% — every a recovered at ~0.70–0.72× its true value; intercepts unbiased. A uniform multiplicative shrinkage of all slopes with unbiased intercepts is the signature of a latent-scale error.
  3. Root cause: make_quadrature() produced an N(0, 2) grid, not N(0, 1). rmutil::gauss.hermite() already returns standard-normal nodes/weights (sum(W)=1, sum(W·P)=0, sum(W·P²)=1). The code additionally multiplied the nodes by sqrt(2) and divided weights by sqrt(pi) (a physicists’-Hermite rescaling that does not apply to rmutil’s output), doubling the trait variance to 2. Integrating ability over SD ≈ √2 shrinks every discrimination by ~1/√2 ≈ 0.707 — exactly the observed ratio.

Why it evaded every prior check: λ-selection is a relative comparison (all three terms share the same quadrature); gradient finite-difference checks pass because the loss and gradient are mutually consistent even with a mis-scaled grid; intercepts are unbiased; and the Louis SE correctly reflects the biased estimator’s sampling distribution. Only ground-truth coverage — comparing point estimates to known true values — could expose it. The pre-existing quadrature unit test checked sum and mean but not variance, so it passed.

Fix (R/quadrature.R): use rmutil::gauss.hermite() nodes/weights directly (they are already standard-normal); normalise weights only for round-off; warn if the realised quadrature variance deviates from 1 (guards against rmutil solver non-convergence, which occurs for large n_quad such as 51).

Verification: quadrature variance is now exactly 1 for n_quad ∈ {7, 11, 21, 31}; discrimination recovery ratio is 1.003 (λ=0) and 1.015 (λ=0.5) at n=3000, vs ~0.70 before.

Test gap closed (tests/testthat/test-core.R): the quadrature test now checks the variance (= 1) across several n_quad, and a new test asserts fit_mixed_subjects_mml recovers discrimination without scale bias on a large sample.

Impact / required follow-up: - This bug affected every function that uses make_quadrature (all MML and frozen-EC fits, posterior weights, ability scoring with default quadrature) — discriminations were systematically ~0.7× throughout the project’s history. - The Scenario 2 coverage run must be re-run with the fix; the existing simulations/results/coverage.rds is invalid. - Scenario 1 (λ selection) conclusions are likely qualitatively unchanged (relative comparison) but should be re-run for the record. Scenario 3 (downstream RMSE) should be re-run since absolute ability-score RMSE depends on the calibration scale. - fit_2pl (mirt) is unaffected — it uses mirt’s own quadrature.

ROBUSTNESS FIX (2026-06-09): tuner could select runaway-discrimination fits

Discovered by the downstream simulation (Scenario 3). R2 (conditional-mean predictor) showed a mean discrimination bias of bias_a = 5.28 — but this was an artifact of 4 catastrophic reps out of 97, not a uniform bias (median bias was 0.026). In those 4 reps the ability-risk tuner selected λ = 0.6–0.7, where the fitted discrimination had run away to 50–200×.

Mechanism (confirmed by per-λ trace on a failing rep):

λ=0.0:  max_a=1.61    risk=0.057   conv=0   ← good
λ=0.3:  max_a=2.05    risk=0.153   conv=0
λ=0.6:  max_a=1035    risk=0.033   conv=0   ← runaway, "converged", LOWEST risk
λ=0.7:  max_a=637     risk=0.040   conv=0   ← runaway, "converged", low risk
λ=0.4,0.5,0.8–1.0:    risk=NaN     conv≠0   ← already filtered

At λ=0.6 the fractional conditional-mean predictor drives the MML objective to a “converged” minimum with discrimination ~1035. The huge implied item information collapses the model-based covariance, so the ability risk g'Σg reads 0.033 — lower than the genuinely-good λ=0 fit (0.057). The tuner minimizes that and picks the garbage fit. The existing filters (non-convergence, non-finite risk) catch the NaN candidates but not these “converged” runaways. On held-out data the runaway fit scores worse (rmse_tuned > rmse_human), confirming the model-based risk was gamed.

Fix (R/risk.R, R/risk-1pl.R): added a max_discrimination argument (default 10) to tune_lambda_ability_risk, tune_lambda_ability_risk_1pl, and tune_lambda_ability_risk_item. Any candidate whose maximum |a| exceeds the bound is treated as degenerate and excluded from selection (its selection_risk is set to Inf). A 2PL discrimination above ~10 is never a real calibration. The full candidate summary (now including a max_disc column) is retained for diagnostics.

Verification: all 4 catastrophic reps now select λ=0 with max_a ≈ 1.3–1.9 and bias_a ≈ −0.08 to 0.09 (was 50–197). New regression test confirms the guard forces λ=0 with a warning when every positive-λ fit exceeds the threshold.

Note: this is a tuner-robustness fix, not a change to the estimator. The underlying pathology — fractional/oracle predictors can make the MML objective unbounded in discrimination at large λ — is real but only matters if a degenerate fit is actually selected. The guard ensures it never is. Users who fix λ manually can still hit the runaway and should set slope_upper in that case.

Required follow-up: re-run Scenario 3 (downstream) with the guard; the existing downstream.rds R2 summary is contaminated by the 4 bad reps.

Phase 0: Diagnostic Results

Setup

Simulation matches the linking-comparison.Rmd vignette:

n_human     = 400  (human calibration sample)
n_generated = 1200 (generated LLM responses)
n_items     = 8    (2PL, a ∈ [0.8, 1.6], d ∈ [-1.1, 1.1])
seed        = 2026
n_quad      = 15

Three predictor types are tested:

Label Construction Theoretical λ*_ppi
exchangeable simulate_2pl(theta_human, true_pars) — independent Bernoulli draws from same DGP ~0 (no gradient covariance)
perfect (F = Y) predicted = observed — exact copy of human responses N/(n+N) = 0.75
conditional_mean plogis(outer(theta, a) + d) — oracle response probabilities (no Bernoulli noise) Should be high, but see finding below

Finding 1: tune_lambda_ppi_score is correctly implemented

Predictor λ*_ppi Theory Match?
exchangeable 0.000 ~0 ✓
perfect (F=Y) 0.750 N/(n+N) = 0.750 ✓ (exact)
conditional_mean 0.000 high ✗ — see Finding 3

The tune_lambda_ppi_score function is numerically correct. The F=Y case recovers the exact theoretical upper bound. The exchangeable case correctly gives 0 because independent binary draws produce near-zero gradient cross-covariance (the control-variate benefit is negligible for i.i.d. noise).

Finding 2: tune_lambda_ability is working correctly for F=Y; λ = 0 for misaligned LLM is NOT a bug

This is the core diagnostic. The reviewer asked: if F=Y still gives λ = 0, there is a bug in the covariance or risk code.

Predictor tune_lambda_ability best_λ Risk trend Interpretation
exchangeable 0 Monotone ↑ Independent draws provide no useful correction
perfect (F=Y) 0.8 U-shaped, min near 0.8 ✓ Correct: more data from same DGP reduces risk
conditional_mean 0 Monotone ↑ Gradient asymmetry causes inflation at any λ > 0
misaligned LLM 0 Monotone ↑ Same gradient asymmetry, stronger magnitude

tune_lambda_ability correctly selects λ = 0.8 for the perfect predictor. This confirms the covariance/risk code is working. The ability risk decreases monotonically to a minimum near λ = 0.8 ≈ N/(n+N) = 0.75 and then rises. The function is operating as intended.

Risk profile for perfect predictor (F=Y):

λ=0.0 → 0.0152  0.1 → 0.0125  0.2 → 0.0101  0.3 → 0.0082
λ=0.4 → 0.0065  0.5 → 0.0053  0.6 → 0.0045  0.7 → 0.0040
λ=0.8 → 0.0040 (min)  0.9 → 0.0045  1.0 → 0.0054

Risk profile for misaligned LLM:

λ=0.0 → 0.0152  0.1 → 0.0171  0.2 → 0.0203  0.3 → 0.0252
λ=0.4 → 0.0323  0.5 → 0.0429  0.6 → 0.0622  0.7 → 0.0860
λ=0.8 → 0.1090  0.9 → 0.1454  1.0 → 0.1808

The misaligned LLM risk increases 12× from λ=0 to λ=1. This is not a code problem — it is the correct mathematical outcome given the frozen expected-count gradient asymmetry.

Finding 3: λ = 0 for conditional_mean is a limitation of the person-level score formula at the human MLE evaluation point

The tune_lambda_ppi_score formula computes:

λ* = Tr(H^-1 (C_hf + C_hf') H^-1) / (2(1+r) Tr(H^-1 V_f H^-1))

For the conditional-mean predictor evaluated at human_pars: - V_f diagonal: 0.016 to 0.035 (nonzero, LLM variance is present) - C_hf diagonal: −0.006 to −0.001 (negative!)

The cross-covariance is negative because human_pars overestimates discrimination for several items (mean ratio = 1.13×, ranging from 0.91× to 1.40×):

Item5:  a_true=1.257, a_human=1.758 (ratio 1.40×)
Item6:  a_true=1.371, a_human=1.610 (ratio 1.17×)
Item2:  a_true=0.914, a_human=1.256 (ratio 1.37×)

When the evaluation point human_pars differs from true_pars, the score function for conditional-mean predictions (which use true_pars probabilities) is evaluated with posteriors from human_pars. The discrepancy creates anti-correlation between the human score S_h and the conditional-mean predictor score S_f. The negative numerator gets clipped to λ = 0.

This is a limitation of the PPI++ score formula when applied at a misspecified evaluation point. It is not a bug; the formula is theoretically correct but requires a good evaluation point (close to the true parameters). For the F=Y case, this limitation is absent because S_f = S_h exactly regardless of the evaluation point.

For the purpose of method validation, the three-test triad should use: - Test A (exchangeable): expected λ*_ppi ≈ 0 → ✓ confirmed - Test B (perfect, F=Y): expected λ*_ppi = N/(n+N) → ✓ confirmed (exactly 0.750) - Test B2 (ability risk, F=Y): expected λ > 0 → ✓ confirmed (0.8)

The conditional-mean test is degenerate for the PPI++ score formula at a misspecified evaluation point and should be replaced in vignettes with the F=Y ability-risk test.

Finding 4: Gradient asymmetry from frozen posteriors is the dominant cause of λ = 0 in vignette scenarios

At the human MLE starting point for the misaligned-LLM scenario, the combined gradient at λ = 0.5 is negative (pushing a upward) for 7 out of 8 items:

Item  ∇L_obs   ∇L_gen  ∇L_pred  Combined (λ=0.5)
Item1 +0.0239  +0.0213  +0.0659   +0.0016   ← barely positive
Item2 +0.0252  +0.0795  +0.1721   -0.0211   ← negative
Item3 +0.0238  +0.0262  +0.0940   -0.0101   ← negative
Item4 +0.0255  +0.0215  +0.1185   -0.0230   ← negative
Item5 +0.0148  +0.0568  +0.1764   -0.0449   ← negative (largest asymmetry)
Item6 +0.0172  +0.0234  +0.1249   -0.0336   ← negative
Item7 +0.0228  +0.0356  +0.1235   -0.0211   ← negative
Item8 +0.0210  +0.0062  +0.1662   -0.0590   ← negative (largest asymmetry)

The asymmetry pattern ∇L_pred >> ∇L_gen is structurally caused by:

  1. L_pred uses concentrated human posteriors (from human_pars which overestimates discrimination) applied to LLM responses. The concentrated posteriors create large residuals N_jk * P_jk - R_jk at the human-ability nodes.

  2. L_gen uses LLM-specific posteriors (more diffuse because LLM discrimination is ~10% lower) applied to LLM responses. Diffuse posteriors give smaller residuals.

  3. ∇L_pred >> ∇L_gen → combined gradient is negative → optimizer pushes a toward the false minimum at large a values.

Consequence: tune_lambda_ability selects λ = 0 because any positive λ moves item parameters toward the false minimum, inflating the sandwich covariance and ability risk. This is the correct mathematical outcome given the current frozen expected-count implementation — λ = 0 is the right answer under this objective. The wrong thing is the objective itself.

The fix: Replace the frozen expected-count objective with the true marginal MML PPI++ objective (fit_mixed_subjects_mml, Phase 2). This eliminates the structural gradient asymmetry because posteriors are recomputed at each gradient evaluation, preventing the self-consistent false minimum.

Phase 0 Summary Table

Predictor λ*_ppi λ*_risk Correct? Root cause if wrong
exchangeable 0 0 ✓ Both correct N/A
perfect (F=Y) 0.75 0.8 ✓ Both correct N/A
conditional_mean 0 0 ppi: limitation; risk: frozen asymmetry (1) negative C_hf at misspecified eval point; (3) gradient asymmetry
misaligned LLM 0 0 ppi: expected; risk: symptom of frozen E-step (3) frozen expected-count gradient asymmetry

The three reviewer-identified causes assign as follows: - Cause 1 (same DGP ≠ useful paired prediction): explains exchangeable = 0. Correct behaviour. - Cause 2 (ability-risk is conservative): Does NOT explain λ = 0 here — ability-risk correctly selects λ = 0.8 for F=Y. Cause 2 is present in principle but is not the dominant factor in these experiments. - Cause 3 (frozen expected-count gradient asymmetry): explains λ = 0 for misaligned LLM and conditional_mean with ability-risk. This is the dominant cause and the target of Phase 2 (MML implementation).

Phase 1: Rename tune_lambda_ability → tune_lambda_ability_risk

Rationale

The reviewer notes: > “Do not use tune_lambda_ability() to validate PPI++ theory. It is a downstream > scoring-risk rule, not the PPI++ oracle coefficient.”

The name tune_lambda_ability is ambiguous — it could be read as “tune lambda using ability [information]” rather than “tune lambda to minimise ability risk”. The rename makes the function’s objective explicit and distinguishes it from tune_lambda_ppi_score.

Changes made

R/risk.R: - Renamed primary function tune_lambda_ability → tune_lambda_ability_risk with full updated documentation. The new docstring explicitly states the minimised objective (E[g′Σ_γg], ability-score risk), distinguishes it from tune_lambda_ppi_score, and includes a diagnostic note explaining why λ = 0 is the correct mathematical outcome for misaligned LLMs under the frozen expected-count implementation. - Renamed tune_lambda_ability_crossfit → tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit. - Updated the internal call inside the crossfit function from tune_lambda_ability to tune_lambda_ability_risk. - Added .Deprecated() wrapper for both old names: tune_lambda_ability() and tune_lambda_ability_crossfit(). These emit a one-time deprecation message and delegate to the new names. All existing code continues to work without modification.

NAMESPACE: - Added tune_lambda_ability_risk and tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit exports. - Kept tune_lambda_ability and tune_lambda_ability_crossfit exports (backward compat).

tests/testthat/test-core.R: - Updated two test calls to the new names to remove deprecation warnings.

Vignettes (lambda-tuning.Rmd, linking-comparison.Rmd, mixed-subjects-workflow.Rmd): - Replaced all tune_lambda_ability( calls with tune_lambda_ability_risk(. - Replaced all tune_lambda_ability_crossfit( calls with tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit(. - Replaced all backtick references (tune_lambda_ability) with new names.

Test results: 33 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN after all changes.

Logic

The two objectives are fundamentally different:

Function Minimises When to use
tune_lambda_ppi_score() Tr(Σ_γ) — item-parameter variance Theoretical validation, method diagnostics
tune_lambda_ability_risk() E[g′Σ_γg] — ability-score risk Practical calibration for operational scoring

Using tune_lambda_ability_risk() for PPI++ theory validation gives misleading results (as Phase 0 shows for the conditional_mean case). Using tune_lambda_ppi_score() for practical scoring decisions ignores the propagation from item parameters to ability estimates. The rename enforces this separation.

Phase 2: fit_mixed_subjects_mml — Marginal MML PPI++

Why it was needed

Phase 0 confirmed the frozen expected-count gradient asymmetry is the dominant cause of λ = 0 in misaligned-LLM scenarios. The gradient ∇L_pred >> ∇L_gen arises because L_pred uses concentrated human-MLE posteriors while L_gen uses diffuse LLM-specific posteriors. This is structural, not fixable by slope caps.

The marginal MML PPI++ objective eliminates this by recomputing posteriors at each gradient evaluation from the current γ. The Bock-Aitkin identity guarantees:

∇_γ L^marg(γ; Y) = expected-count gradient with posteriors from current γ

The true marginal likelihood has a unique minimum at the MML optimum. The false minimum that exists in the frozen objective (where bimodal posteriors at a=4.66 are self-consistent with the frozen expected counts) does NOT exist in the marginal objective — at a=4.66, the true marginal gradient for human data pushes a back down toward the human MLE because the human responses from true a=1.26 are genuinely inconsistent with a=4.66 in the marginal sense.

Changes made

R/quadrature.R — Added posterior_and_log_lik_2pl(resp, item_pars, quadrature): - Internal helper (not exported) that computes posterior weights AND per-subject log-normalizers log Z_i = log[Σ_k A_k p(Y_i|θ_k; γ)] - Identical logic to posterior_weights_2pl but saves the logsumexp before normalising - Returns list(weights = [n×k], log_normalizers = [n])

R/internal.R — Added two internal helpers: - marginal_loss_2pl(resp, item_pars, quadrature): true IRT negative marginal log- likelihood = −mean(log Z_i). Differs from the frozen expected-count loss: this is the actual log p(Y|γ) rather than the Q-function lower bound. - marginal_gradient_2pl(resp, item_pars, quadrature): gradient of marginal_loss_2pl via the Bock-Aitkin identity — recomputes posteriors from current item_pars at each call. Verified against finite differences (max error < 1e-10).

R/fit.R — Added fit_mixed_subjects_mml(...): - Minimises L_obs^marg + λ*(L_gen^marg − L_pred^marg) where all three are true marginal likelihoods - Objective and gradient share a per-call cache so posteriors are computed at most once per (par, dataset) combination per L-BFGS-B step - mml_pred_weights = "own" (default): L_pred uses its own posteriors — fully consistent objective and gradient; eliminates the frozen-posterior asymmetry - mml_pred_weights = "human": L_pred uses human posteriors (EM approximation for the chain rule term); retains paired-ability information - After convergence, stores final posteriors at the converged γ in q_observed, q_predicted, q_generated, so vcov_mixed_subjects() computes the correct marginal sandwich covariance without any additional function - slope_upper is not required for stability (no false minima in the marginal objective) - Print method shows “estimator: marginal MML PPI++” - Exported and documented

R/risk.R — Added fit_fn argument to tune_lambda_ability_risk(): - Defaults to fit_mixed_subjects (unchanged existing behaviour) - Pass fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects_mml to use the MML estimator throughout the lambda grid search

NAMESPACE — Added fit_mixed_subjects_mml export.

tests/testthat/test-core.R — Added 3 new tests: 1. Gradient check: marginal_gradient_2pl matches finite differences to 1e-5 2. Basic MML fit: converges, returns valid object, vcov_mixed_subjects works 3. F=Y validation: tune_lambda_ability_risk(..., fit_fn=fit_mixed_subjects_mml) selects λ > 0 for F=Y — the defining test of the Phase 2 fix

Validation results

The Phase 2 implementation passes all diagnostics:

Predictor tune_lambda_ability_risk (frozen, slope_upper=4) tune_lambda_ability_risk (MML)
F=Y 0.8 0.7
Misaligned LLM 0 0

MML with misaligned LLM: - All convergence codes = 0 (no slope_upper required) - Max discrimination values grow smoothly (1.4 to 2.4 over λ ∈ [0,1]) instead of diverging to the false minimum - Correctly selects λ = 0 (LLM is misaligned; using it does not help) - Risk increases 3.7× from λ=0 to λ=1 (vs 12× for frozen estimator) — problem is well-conditioned

MML with F=Y (λ=0.7): - Risk profile is U-shaped, minimum near theoretical N/(n+N) = 0.75 - The false minimum is absent: discriminations converge to MLE without slope capping

Key insight confirmed: λ = 0 for misaligned LLM is the CORRECT mathematical outcome even with MML. The MML objective is a well-posed combination of human and LLM marginal likelihoods; when the LLM is misaligned, the correction still adds noise to ability estimates and the risk is minimised at λ = 0. The difference from the frozen estimator is that the risk surface is smooth and well-conditioned — the false minimum is gone.

No separate vcov_mixed_subjects_mml needed

The existing vcov_mixed_subjects() works correctly for MML fits because the output object stores posteriors at the CONVERGED parameters (not frozen initial posteriors). The sandwich covariance computed from these posteriors is the asymptotically valid marginal sandwich for the MML estimator.

Test results

43 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN.

Phase 3: Per-item lambda

Motivation

A global scalar λ is fragile: one subset of items with poor LLM predictions forces the global optimum to λ = 0, preventing well-predicted items from benefiting from the LLM data. Per-item λ_j allows each item’s correction weight to match the quality of the paired LLM predictions for that item.

Implementation

R/internal.R — Added item_loss_and_grad(counts, item_pars): - Returns per-item contributions to the expected-count loss (n_items-vector) and per-item gradients (n_items-vector for a, n_items-vector for d) - Verified: sum(item_loss) == loss_expected_counts(...) and c(grad_a, grad_d) == gradient_expected_counts(...) (equality up to 1e-10) - Used by fit_mixed_subjects_mml() vector-lambda path

R/fit.R — Updated fit_mixed_subjects_mml() to accept lambda as a scalar OR length-n_items vector: - Scalar path (Phase 2 unchanged): true marginal likelihoods; posteriors recomputed at every gradient evaluation; fully consistent objective and gradient - Vector path (Phase 3 new): frozen expected-count objective with per-item λ_j; posteriors frozen at initial_pars (same mechanism as fit_mixed_subjects()); the frozen Q-function and its gradient are fully consistent — no code 52 convergence failures; item_loss_and_grad() applies each λ_j independently to its item’s contribution

Design rationale: the consistent-objective requirement forces the vector path to use frozen posteriors (the Q-function lower bound). The EM approximation (gradient = ∂L^marg/∂γ, not ∂Q/∂γ) would be inconsistent with the Q-function objective, causing L-BFGS-B line-search failures. Per-item tuning from tune_lambda_ability_risk_item() assigns λ_j ≈ 0 to items where the LLM correction is harmful, containing the frozen-posterior gradient asymmetry to items where it is actually beneficial.

R/risk.R — Added two new exported functions:

tune_lambda_ppi_score_item(observed, predicted, item_pars, n_generated, ...): - Applies the PPI++ Proposition 2 formula independently per item using the 2×2 diagonal block of H^{-1} for item j and the 2D sub-vectors of S_h, S_f - Fast: O(1 posterior computation + matrix operations), no fitting required - Returns a vector of per-item λ*_j values - Verified: F=Y gives λ*_j = N/(n+N) for all j exactly ✓ - Intended use: quick diagnostic for which items benefit from LLM predictions

tune_lambda_ability_risk_item(lambda_grid, ..., init_lambda = 0, n_pass = 1): - Coordinate descent: for each item j, find the λ_j ∈ lambda_grid that minimises ability-score risk while holding all other λ_{j’} fixed - init_lambda: starting vector (scalar broadcast to all items). Recommended practice: pass the global scalar optimum from tune_lambda_ability_risk() as init_lambda so the search starts near a useful operating point rather than all-zeros (where individual per-item improvements are hard to detect) - n_pass = 1: single greedy sweep (sufficient in practice; increase for refinement) - O(n_items × length(lambda_grid) × fitting_cost) per pass

Key validation results

tune_lambda_ppi_score_item with F=Y (all items):
  Item1  Item2  Item3  Item4  Item5  Item6  Item7  Item8
  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750  0.750
  (all equal N/(n+N) = 0.75 ✓)

fit_mixed_subjects_mml with vector lambda (all-0.75, F=Y):
  Convergence: 0   max_a: 1.572   (no slope_upper needed)

fit_mixed_subjects_mml with partial lambda ([0.5]*4, [0]*4):
  Convergence: 0   max_a: 1.683
  Items with lambda=0: a values close to human-only MLE
  Items with lambda=0.5: a values slightly shifted toward combined estimate

Recommended workflow for per-item lambda

# Step 1: Find global scalar optimum
global <- tune_lambda_ability_risk(
  lambda_grid = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1),
  observed = observed, predicted = predicted, generated = generated,
  fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects_mml, ...
)

# Step 2: Refine per-item starting from global optimum
item_tuned <- tune_lambda_ability_risk_item(
  lambda_grid = seq(0, 1, by = 0.2),
  observed = observed, predicted = predicted, generated = generated,
  init_lambda = global$best_lambda,  # start from global best
  ...
)

# Step 3: Inspect per-item lambda
print(data.frame(item = item_tuned$item, lambda = round(item_tuned$lambda, 3)))

# Step 4: Use final fit for scoring
vcov_item <- vcov_mixed_subjects(item_tuned$final_fit)

Test results

52 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN.

Round 2 (reviewer follow-up): Steps 1–3

Step 1: Correct marginal-MML sandwich covariance (vcov_mixed_subjects_mml)

The problem. vcov_mixed_subjects() uses avg_hessian_counts() — the EM complete-data Hessian — as the bread. For the frozen expected-count estimator this is correct. For the marginal-MML estimator (fit_mixed_subjects_mml) it is not: the complete-data Hessian over-states information by ignoring the missing information about the latent ability θ (Louis 1982).

The fix — Louis’ identity. The correct bread for the MML estimator is the marginal observed information:

A_marg = H_comp - I_miss

where I_miss = (1/n) Σ_i Var_{w_i}(s_ik) is the average within-posterior variance of the complete-data score vector at each node. The meat (marginal score outer products) is already correct in the existing implementation.

Implementation (R/risk.R):

  • Added louis_missing_info(resp, weights, item_pars) (internal helper). For each pair of items (j, l), computes the three second-moment matrices (aa, ad, dd) via a fully vectorised decomposition:

    sm_block = 1/n × [yy - yp - t(yp) + pp]

    where yy, yp, pp involve outer products of response columns with expected- probability matrices. Cost: O(J² × n × K) — fast (≈800K ops for J=8, n=400, K=31). The formula was verified against numerical central-difference Hessian to < 6×10⁻¹⁰ error across all six parameters.

  • Added vcov_mixed_subjects_mml(object, ridge = 1e-8) (exported). Bread: louis_bread(q_X) = avg_hessian_counts(q_X) - louis_missing_info(q_X) for each of the three datasets, combined with the usual + λ*(gen - pred) weighting. Meat: unchanged (marginal score covariance).

  • Updated vcov.mixedsubjects_fit S3 dispatch: routes to vcov_mixed_subjects_mml when isTRUE(object$mml) && length(object$lambda) == 1, and to the existing vcov_mixed_subjects otherwise.

Empirical effect. For the standard vignette scenario (n=400, N=1200, F=Y): - Mean diagonal of MML sandwich: 0.0074 (Louis-corrected) - Mean diagonal of EM sandwich: 0.0032 (over-precise)

The ~2.3× larger MML uncertainty correctly accounts for the missing information about individual abilities. The EM bread treats the latent ability as observed, which inflates efficiency.

Note. The marginal Hessian is only guaranteed positive definite AT the MLE. At the true parameters (which may differ from the sample MLE), it can be indefinite — this is the correct mathematical behaviour, not a bug.

Step 2: Fix mml_pred_weights = "human" objective/gradient mismatch

The problem. With mml_pred_weights = "human", the recompute closure in fit_mixed_subjects_mml was calling summarize_expected_counts(predicted, obs_r$weights) where obs_r was recomputed from the CURRENT candidate ip at each L-BFGS-B evaluation. The objective changes with ip through both the item parameters in the loss AND through the human posteriors used to weight the predicted term. But the gradient only accounted for the first channel (direct gradient, EM approximation). This mismatch meant fn and gr were inconsistent, causing L-BFGS-B line-search failures.

The fix. When mml_pred_weights = "human", compute the human posteriors ONCE from init_std and freeze them. The resulting objective loss_expected_counts(pred_cts_fixed, ip) depends on ip only through the item parameters in the loss (not through the weights), so the EM gradient gradient_expected_counts(pred_cts_fixed, ip) IS the gradient of the objective. Fully consistent.

This is the fixed-nuisance Q-function interpretation: human posteriors are a fixed nuisance, not a parameter.

The final q_predicted stored in the output also uses pred_w_fixed so that vcov_mixed_subjects_mml uses consistent posteriors for the bread and meat.

Step 3a: Convergence and finite-risk filtering in tune_lambda_ability_risk

Added selection_risk column to the summary table: candidates with non-zero convergence codes or non-finite risk get selection_risk = Inf. If all candidates fail, a warning is issued and lambda = 0 (human-only) is returned. The full summary is retained for diagnostics.

Applied to: tune_lambda_ability_risk, tune_lambda_ability_risk_1pl.

Step 3b: Cross-fit target mode and final-fit function

target_mode argument added to tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit: - "fixed" (default): the full target_resp is used in every fold. Correct for operational scoring where the target population is independent of the labeled split. When target_resp is not supplied, defaults to "row_aligned" for backward compatibility. - "row_aligned": only the training rows of target_resp are used per fold. Valid when target_resp = observed and fold-matched evaluation is desired.

final_fit_fn argument added to tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit (default: fit_mixed_subjects_split). Allows users to pass any final-fitting function. Note that mixing MML fold tuning with a frozen EC final fit is an approximation; document this when reporting results.

Step 3c: vcov_mixed_subjects for vector lambda

validate_lambda(object$lambda) was replaced by validate_lambda_vector(...) to accept vector lambda. For vector lambda: - Bread: item j’s 2×2 diagonal block uses λ_j (from the block-diagonal frozen EC Hessian). Off-diagonal coupling terms are scaled by √(λ_j λ_l). - Meat: item j’s score columns are scaled by λ_j in the labeled score matrix; generated score columns are scaled similarly.

Test count after Round 2: 75 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN

New tests added: - louis_missing_info matches numerical Hessian to < 1e-5; MLE bread is PD - vcov dispatches correctly (MML gets Louis bread, EC gets EM bread; MML diagonal > EC diagonal) - vcov handles vector lambda without error - tune_lambda_ability_risk selection_risk column filters failed candidates

Round 3 (Steps 4–7): Documentation and vignette restructuring

Step 4: Docstring updates

  • fit_mixed_subjects_mml (R/fit.R): expanded @details to explicitly document (a) that mml_pred_weights = "human" uses a fixed-nuisance Q-function (not a true marginal objective) with frozen posteriors from init_std, and

    1. that vector lambda (per-item) also uses a frozen Q-function and should be reported as approximate.

  • tune_lambda_ability_risk_item (R/risk.R): added @note block stating that coordinate descent uses the frozen Q-function, explaining the approximation conditions, and recommending that results be labelled experimental.

  • vcov_mixed_subjects_1pl (R/risk-1pl.R): added @note that the bread uses the EM complete-data Hessian rather than the Louis correction, and flags the Louis-corrected 1PL bread as planned for a future release.

Step 5: Vignette rewrites

vignettes/mixed-subjects-workflow.Rmd (full rewrite): - Primary workflow now uses fit_mixed_subjects_mml throughout. - tune_lambda_ability_risk(..., fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects_mml) is shown as the recommended lambda-selection step with Louis-corrected covariance. - Removed slope_upper workaround from the default workflow. - Added a “When the LLM is a good predictor” section using F = Y to verify that the method selects λ>0\lambda > 0 when appropriate.

vignettes/lambda-tuning.Rmd (full rewrite): - Restructured around a clean five-row table: ppi_score, MML ability-risk (recommended), frozen EC (fast approximation), crossfit, per-item. - Explains the two objectives (Tr(Σ_γ) vs E[g’Σg]) and two estimators (frozen EC vs MML) with the key practical distinction. - tune_lambda_ability_risk(..., fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects_mml) is labelled “Recommended default”. - Explains selection_risk filtering and the target_mode / final_fit_fn crossfit arguments. - Notes that frozen EC requires slope_upper while MML does not.

vignettes/linking-comparison.Rmd (retitled + new section): - Title changed to “IRT Linking and Gradient Asymmetry: Diagnostic Guide”. - Added final section “The marginal-MML fix” showing a direct comparison of frozen EC (with slope_upper = 4) vs MML at lambda = 0.2, and an MML lambda sweep confirming no slope cap is needed. - Recommendation section updated to direct users to fit_mixed_subjects_mml for the recommended workflow while keeping the linking analysis as a diagnostic.

Step 6: New vignettes

vignettes/lambda-tuning-item.Rmd (new, experimental): - Motivates per-item lambda with a heterogeneous test (4 good + 4 poor items). - Shows tune_lambda_ppi_score_item() as the fast diagnostic step. - Shows tune_lambda_ability_risk_item(init_lambda = global_best) as the coordinate-descent step. - Explains why starting from all-zeros is not recommended. - Clearly labels all results as approximate/experimental.

vignettes/mixed-subjects-1pl.Rmd (new): - Introduces the 1PL model, explains when to prefer it over 2PL. - Shows fit_1pl(), fit_mixed_subjects_mml_1pl(), vcov_mixed_subjects_1pl(). - Shows tune_lambda_ability_risk_1pl() and verifies F = Y gives λ>0\lambda > 0. - Includes 1PL vs 2PL comparison on a correctly-specified 1PL test. - Notes that the 1PL vcov uses the EM bread (not Louis-corrected).

Test count after Round 3: 75 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN (unchanged)

Round 4 (third reviewer pass): Bug fixes

Eight issues were identified in the third static review. The reviewer confirmed that all Round 2 and Round 3 fixes landed correctly. Two remaining issues were described as “important implementation bugs that should be fixed before trusting simulation results.”

Assessment

The reviewer’s ranking was accurate. Issue 1 (vector-lambda quadrature summaries) was a genuine correctness bug — if left unaddressed, vcov() on a per-item-lambda fit would compute the bread and meat from posteriors that the optimizer never used, making the sandwich covariance inconsistent with the actual estimating equation. Issue 2 (... leakage) was a latent API fragility: passing fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects_mml via ... to tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit would silently forward that argument to final_fit_fn, causing an unused-argument error or unexpected behaviour for any final_fit_fn that doesn’t have a fit_fn parameter. Issues 3–5 were documentation and test quality problems with no effect on numerical results. The remaining three issues (1PL Louis correction, missingness in Louis formula, EAP ability risk) were already acknowledged as deferred work.

Fix 1: Vector-lambda quadrature summaries now store frozen posteriors (R/fit.R)

Bug. After optimization in fit_mixed_subjects_mml(), the output quadrature summaries (q_observed, q_predicted, q_generated) were always built from the converged parameters conv_pars, regardless of whether a scalar or vector lambda was used. For the vector-lambda (frozen Q-function) path, the optimizer used expected counts frozen at init_std, but the stored summaries were at conv_pars. When vcov_mixed_subjects() was subsequently called, it computed Hessian and score vectors from these mismatched posteriors, making the sandwich covariance inconsistent with the estimating equation that determined conv_pars.

Fix. Added a branch on scalar_lambda in the final summary construction:

  • Scalar path (unchanged): summaries built at conv_pars, so vcov_mixed_subjects_mml() (Louis-corrected) is consistent.
  • Vector path (fixed): summaries built from init_std as item parameters and obs_w_frozen/gen_w_frozen as posterior weights — exactly the frozen posteriors that were used during optimization. vcov_mixed_subjects() (EM bread) is now consistent with the frozen Q-function.

Verified: vector-lambda q_observed$irt_pars$a now exactly matches init_pars$a.

Fix 2: Explicit fit_fn argument in tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit (R/risk.R)

Bug. tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit had no explicit fit_fn parameter. When users passed fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects_mml it traveled through ..., which was forwarded to both the fold-level tune_lambda_ability_risk calls (where it was needed) and to final_fit_fn via list(...) in do.call (where it caused an unused-argument error or unexpected behaviour for functions like fit_mixed_subjects_split that have no fit_fn parameter).

Fix. Added fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects as an explicit named argument to tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit. The fold-loop passes fit_fn = fit_fn explicitly. The do.call for final_fit_fn no longer includes list(...) — fold-tuning arguments do not contaminate the final fit call.

The consequence: any user-supplied ... arguments (e.g. slope_upper) are now forwarded to the fold tuner but NOT to the final fitter. Both are documented in the updated @param block.

Fix 3: Stale @return sentence in fit_mixed_subjects_mml (R/fit.R)

Updated the @return section to correctly describe the dispatch behaviour:

  • Scalar lambda: stats::vcov() dispatches to vcov_mixed_subjects_mml() (Louis-corrected marginal sandwich). Calling vcov_mixed_subjects() directly bypasses the Louis correction.
  • Vector lambda: summaries now store frozen posteriors; stats::vcov() dispatches to vcov_mixed_subjects() (EM bread), consistent with the frozen Q-function.

The previous text pointed users to vcov_mixed_subjects() for the “correct marginal sandwich covariance,” which was wrong — the correct route is always stats::vcov().

Fix 4: MML covariance test updated to use stats::vcov() (tests/testthat/test-core.R)

The existing test for MML covariance dispatch called vcov_mixed_subjects(fit), which deliberately tests the EM-bread path and does not exercise the Louis correction. Replaced with:

  1. stats::vcov(fit_mml) — verifies the S3 dispatch routes to Louis-corrected.
  2. Equality check: stats::vcov(fit_mml) == vcov_mixed_subjects_mml(fit_mml).
  3. Ordering check: stats::vcov(fit_mml) mean diagonal > stats::vcov(fit_ec) mean diagonal (Louis > EM).
  4. Bypass check: vcov_mixed_subjects(fit_mml) mean diagonal < stats::vcov(fit_mml) mean diagonal — confirms that calling the EM-bread function directly gives smaller (over-precise) uncertainty for an MML fit.

Fix 5: Per-item lambda description corrected (R/risk.R)

The tune_lambda_ability_risk_item() description said “uses fit_mixed_subjects_mml() at each candidate, so posteriors are recomputed from the current parameters.” This contradicted the accurate @note block that correctly describes the frozen Q-function approximation.

Updated the description to: “calls fit_mixed_subjects_mml() with a per-item lambda vector at each candidate evaluation. Because the lambda is a vector, that function switches to its frozen expected-count Q-function path — posteriors are frozen at initial_pars, not recomputed continuously.”

Issues acknowledged as deferred (not yet fixed)

Issue Status
Louis missing-information correction for item-level missingness vcov_mixed_subjects_mml warns on NA; full mask-based correction deferred
1PL marginal bread (Louis correction) Documented as planned; 1PL vcov uses EM bread
EAP-based ability risk (more stable at boundaries) Deferred; ML boundary guard added as interim fix
Split-sample vcov() Throws informative error directing users to fit_mixed_subjects_mml

Test count after Round 4: 77 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN

Two new tests added (expanded MML covariance dispatch test); no tests removed.

Round 5 (fourth reviewer pass)

Assessment

The reviewer confirmed that Fixes 1–3 from Round 4 are correct and all Round 2/3 changes landed as expected. The one substantive issue identified as “still present” (Issues 1 and 2) reflected the reviewer examining the GitHub remote, which at that point had not yet received the Round 4 commits. The local code already contained those fixes.

One genuinely new issue was identified: 1PL tuning robustness — the tune_lambda_ability_risk_1pl() loop had no tryCatch protection around the fit, covariance, or risk calls. A single failed candidate could abort the entire tuning call before the selection_risk filter had a chance to handle it, unlike the 2PL tuner which wraps each step defensively.

Fix: 1PL tuner robustness (R/risk-1pl.R)

Added the same tryCatch pattern used in tune_lambda_ability_risk():

  1. The fit_fn(...) call is wrapped; a failed fit returns NULL and the row gets convergence = 99L and mean_total_risk = Inf.
  2. vcov_mixed_subjects_1pl() is wrapped; a failed covariance (singular bread, degenerate item parameters) returns NULL and the risk is set to Inf.
  3. ability_risk_1pl() is wrapped; any remaining failure returns Inf risk.

The existing selection_risk filter then excludes all Inf-risk candidates before which.min is called, and the existing warning fires when all candidates fail. The behaviour is now identical to the 2PL tuner.

Remaining deferred issues (unchanged from Round 4)

Issue Status
Louis missing-information correction for item-level missingness vcov_mixed_subjects_mml warns on NA; full mask deferred
1PL marginal bread (Louis correction) EM bread documented as approximate; deferred
EAP-based ability risk ML boundary guard added; EAP deferred
Split-sample vcov() Throws informative error; deferred

Test count after Round 5: 77 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN (unchanged)

Round 6 (fifth reviewer pass): Sign-off + path to simulation validation

Assessment of the feedback

The reviewer signed off on the scalar 2PL MML + ability-risk workflow as “internally consistent enough to move into simulation validation.” This matches our own read of the state: the marginal objective, the Louis-corrected covariance dispatch, ability-risk tuning, crossfit target handling, candidate filtering, and the vignette restructuring are all in place and tested (77 passing tests).

The reviewer raised five caveats. Our evaluation:

# Caveat Verdict Action
1 Crossfit cannot pass args to the final fit (the ... leak was fixed by forwarding nothing) Valid. Real ergonomic gap — slope_upper, mml_pred_weights cannot reach final_fit_fn. Add tuning_args / final_args (Task A)
2 Test line 459 calls vcov_mixed_subjects() directly on an MML fit Valid. Confirmed; misleading comment too. Switch to vcov() dispatch + add explicit frozen-EC path label (Task B)
3 mml_pred_weights = "human" covariance approximate Already documented honestly None
4 Per-item lambda is frozen-Q, not full MML Already documented; keep flagged experimental None
5 1PL covariance uses EM bread Already documented; deferred None

Where we go beyond the reviewer’s suggestions: their four λ-selection scenarios are necessary but not sufficient for a methods paper. Two checks matter more now that the Louis bread is implemented and are not in their list:

  • SE coverage. The entire purpose of the Louis correction was honest uncertainty. We must verify the Louis-corrected item-parameter and ability-score intervals attain nominal coverage in simulation. Without this, the correction is unvalidated.
  • Downstream payoff + no-harm. The method’s value proposition is lower ability-score RMSE when the predictor is informative, and no worse than human-only when it is not. This is the headline result for the paper and must be measured directly, not inferred from λ alone.

Plan

Task A — Crossfit argument separation (ergonomics, ~30 min)

Replace the single ... in tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit() with explicit tuning_args = list() and final_args = list(). Fold-level tuning receives c(core_tuning_args, tuning_args); the final fit receives c(core_final_args, extra_split, final_args). Keep ... deprecated-but-accepted, routing it into tuning_args with a one-time message, so existing calls do not break. Document both lists with examples (slope_upper to tuning, mml_pred_weights to final).

Task B — Test cleanup (correctness habit, ~15 min)

In tests/testthat/test-core.R line ~459 (the fit_mixed_subjects_mml basic-fit test): change vcov_mixed_subjects(fit) to stats::vcov(fit) and update the comment to “vcov() dispatches to the Louis-corrected MML covariance.” Confirm the deliberate frozen-EC path at line ~630 keeps its explanatory comment. Line ~338 is a genuine fit_mixed_subjects() (frozen EC) fit and correctly stays as vcov_mixed_subjects().

Task C — Simulation validation harness (the main work)

Build a reproducible simulation study under a new top-level simulations/ directory (standalone scripts, not built into the package, to keep CRAN check fast). Structure:

  • simulations/dgp.R — shared data-generating helpers (true 2PL pars; the five predictor regimes below).
  • simulations/run_lambda_selection.R — Scenario set 1 (λ behaviour).
  • simulations/run_coverage.R — Scenario set 2 (SE coverage).
  • simulations/run_downstream.R — Scenario set 3 (RMSE payoff + no-harm).
  • simulations/figures.R — render summary plots/tables from saved .rds.

Predictor regimes (extends the reviewer’s four):

Regime Construction Expected ability-risk λ*
R1 perfect (F=Y) predicted = observed λ > 0, near N/(n+N)
R2 conditional mean plogis(a θ + d) (oracle probs) high
R3 same-DGP draw fresh simulate_2pl(θ_human, true) modest, > 0 but well below R1
R4 independent noise simulate_2pl(θ_human, scrambled) ≈ 0
R5 LLM shift attenuated a, shifted d (vignette LLM) shrinks toward 0

Scenario set 1 — λ selection (50–100 reps each). For each regime, fit with fit_mixed_subjects_mml over a λ grid, tune by tune_lambda_ability_risk, record selected λ. Assert the qualitative ordering λ(R1) ≳ λ(R2) > λ(R3) > λ(R5) ≈ λ(R4) ≈ 0. Report the full selected-λ distribution, not just the mean — the same-DGP regime (R3) is the subtle one and deserves a histogram.

Scenario set 2 — Louis SE coverage (≥200 reps). Under R1, R3, and R5 with a known true gamma, compute Wald intervals from vcov(fit) for each item parameter and for a set of fixed ability-score targets. Report empirical coverage of nominal 90% / 95% intervals. Compare against the EM-bread covariance (vcov_mixed_subjects directly) to demonstrate the Louis correction restores coverage that the EM bread under-covers. This is the key validation of Round 2’s Louis work.

Scenario set 3 — downstream payoff + no-harm (≥100 reps). Hold out a scoring sample with known theta_true. Compare ability-score RMSE for: (a) human-only (λ=0), (b) MML at tuned λ, (c) MML at λ=1 (full PPI). Assert: - R1/R2: tuned-λ RMSE < human-only RMSE (predictor helps). - R4/R5: tuned-λ RMSE ≤ human-only RMSE + tolerance (no harm — the criterion correctly declines a useless predictor). Also record item-parameter bias to confirm the MML estimator is unbiased at λ chosen by the criterion.

Deliverables: saved .rds result objects, a short simulations/README.md describing how to reproduce, and 3–4 summary figures. Optionally distil a small-rep (≈10) version into a simulation-validation.Rmd vignette for the docs site, gated so it does not slow the CRAN build.

Task D — Wire results back into docs (after C)

Once Scenario sets 1–3 produce stable results, add a one-paragraph “Validation” section to the workflow vignette summarising the headline findings (λ ordering, coverage, RMSE payoff) with a pointer to simulations/.

Sequencing

Tasks A and B are quick and unblock nothing downstream — do them first to clear the reviewer’s actionable caveats. Task C is the substantive next phase and is where method risk actually lives; within it, Scenario set 2 (coverage) is the highest-value check because it directly tests the Louis correction that this round of work centered on. Task D is documentation polish that depends on C.

Tasks A and B — COMPLETED

Task B (test cleanup). tests/testthat/test-core.R: the fit_mixed_subjects_mml basic-fit test now calls stats::vcov(fit) (was vcov_mixed_subjects(fit)) with an updated comment. The deliberate frozen-EC bypass test and the genuine frozen-EC fit test are unchanged.

Task A (crossfit argument separation). tune_lambda_ability_risk_crossfit() now takes explicit tuning_args = list() and final_args = list():

  • tuning_args is spliced into each fold-level tune_lambda_ability_risk() call (e.g. tuning_args = list(slope_upper = 4)).
  • final_args is spliced into the final_fit_fn call only (e.g. final_args = list(mml_pred_weights = "own")).
  • Legacy ... is still accepted: it is routed into tuning_args with a one-time deprecation message, so existing calls do not break.
  • Both do.call sites now splice the appropriate list; tuning-only arguments (fit_fn, tuning_args) can no longer reach final_fit_fn.

Added a regression test (crossfit separates tuning_args and final_args) verifying that both lists route correctly, that the MML final fit collapses fold lambdas to a scalar, and that the legacy ... path fires the deprecation message.

Test count after Tasks A+B: 81 PASS, 0 FAIL, 0 WARN.

Task C — COMPLETED: simulation validation harness

Built a standalone simulation harness under simulations/ (excluded from the package build via .Rbuildignore; generated artifacts excluded via simulations/.gitignore).

Files:

File Purpose
simulations/dgp.R True 2PL parameters; five predictor regimes (R1–R5); generate_regime()
simulations/run_lambda_selection.R Scenario 1: λ selection across regimes
simulations/run_coverage.R Scenario 2: Louis vs EM SE coverage (the key check)
simulations/run_downstream.R Scenario 3: ability-score RMSE payoff + no-harm
simulations/figures.R Render tables/figures from saved .rds
simulations/README.md Reproduction guide + interpretation notes

Five predictor regimes (ordered best→worst): R1 perfect (F=Y), R2 conditional mean (oracle probabilities), R3 same-DGP draw, R4 independent noise, R5 LLM shift. Each run_*.R takes the rep count as a CLI argument.

Design decisions worth recording:

  • Coverage restricted to R1 and R3. Coverage of the true DGP parameters is only meaningful where the combined estimator is consistent for true_pars. That holds for R1 (paired = observed, generated from true) and R3 (both from true) at any λ, but NOT for R5 at λ > 0 (generated comes from shifted parameters → biased estimand). The coverage script therefore uses a fixed λ = 0.5 on R1/R3 only.
  • Louis vs EM contrast. The coverage script compares stats::vcov(fit) (Louis-corrected, via dispatch) against vcov_mixed_subjects(fit) (EM bread, deliberate bypass) on the same fits, isolating the effect of the Louis correction.

Smoke-test findings (NOT inferential — tiny rep counts): All three scenarios execute end-to-end and produce qualitatively correct output.

  • Scenario 1 (2 reps): selected λ ordering was R1 ≈ 0.70 (PPI score 0.75 ≈ N/(n+N)) > R3 ≈ 0.25 > R2 = R4 = R5 = 0. R3 ≈ 0.25 matches the Bernoulli noise-floor prediction from the Phase 0 diagnostic.
  • Scenario 2 (3 reps): Louis SEs ≈ 1.6× wider than EM SEs, and Louis coverage exceeded EM coverage in every cell — the correct qualitative direction. The full 200-rep run is needed for absolute coverage numbers.
  • Scenario 3 (reduced config): produced interpretable RMSE comparisons across regimes.

Notable scientific finding to follow up in the full run: R2 (oracle conditional-mean predictor) selected λ ≈ 0, not the naively-expected high value. This reproduces the Phase 0 diagnostic: at the human-MLE evaluation point, the gradient cross-covariance between the human score and a deterministic conditional-mean prediction is near zero, so the control-variate benefit does not appear in the person-level score formulation. The full λ-selection run will quantify how often this occurs. This is documented in simulations/README.md.

Compute note: the downstream scenario is expensive (each rep does 11 grid-point MML tunes, each fitting MML + Louis vcov + scoring the target sample), so full runs are intended to be launched by the user with the rep count as a CLI argument; smoke tests confirm execution only.

Task D — COMPLETED (2026-06-09)

Full runs executed (λ-selection 100 reps, coverage 200 reps, downstream 100 reps), all post-quadrature-fix and post-runaway-guard. Headline results:

  • λ selection: R1 perfect → 0.750 (= N/(n+N)); R4 noise → ~0; R3/R5 → ~0.10; R2 conditional-mean → ~0 (documented oracle-predictor caveat).
  • Coverage (the key result): Louis-corrected Wald intervals attain nominal coverage for BOTH parameter types — discrimination a 0.91/0.96, intercept d 0.91/0.95 — while the EM bread under-covers (a ~0.60, d ~0.84). This validates the Louis marginal-information bread end-to-end. (Pre-quadrature-fix, a coverage was 0.04; the fix raised it to 0.91.)
  • Downstream: no-harm holds everywhere (tuned RMSE ≤ human-only); discrimination unbiased at the selected λ (|bias_a| ≤ 0.04); RMSE gains modest because the 8-item test is measurement-limited (the larger payoff is in item-parameter precision, i.e. the coverage result).

Deliverables: - New vignette vignettes/simulation-validation.Rmd presents and interprets all three scenarios with static tables (embeds the actual numbers so it builds without the gitignored .rds files; verified it knits and the R chunks execute). - vignettes/mixed-subjects-workflow.Rmd gained a short “Validation” section summarising the three findings with a pointer to the new vignette and to simulations/.

Items intentionally deferred (unchanged)

Louis correction under item-level missingness; Louis-corrected 1PL bread; EAP-based ability risk; split-sample vcov(). None block simulation validation of the scalar 2PL workflow.

Files modified in Phase 1 and Phase 2

File Change
R/quadrature.R Added posterior_and_log_lik_2pl (internal, Phase 2)
R/internal.R Added marginal_loss_2pl, marginal_gradient_2pl (Phase 2); item_loss_and_grad (Phase 3)
R/fit.R Added fit_mixed_subjects_mml (Phase 2); updated to accept vector lambda (Phase 3); mml flag in print method
R/risk.R Renamed to tune_lambda_ability_risk / _crossfit (Phase 1); deprecated old names; added fit_fn argument (Phase 2); added tune_lambda_ppi_score_item, tune_lambda_ability_risk_item, init_lambda argument (Phase 3)
NAMESPACE Added all new exports across Phases 1–3
tests/testthat/test-core.R Updated old names (Phase 1); 3 MML tests (Phase 2); 3 per-item lambda tests (Phase 3) — 52 total
vignettes/lambda-tuning.Rmd Updated function calls to new names
vignettes/linking-comparison.Rmd Updated function calls to new names
vignettes/mixed-subjects-workflow.Rmd Updated function calls to new names
2026-06-04-changes.md This file

On this page

Developed by Klint Kanopka.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
